title: Mortgage Renewal: 6 Things to Do Before You Sign
metaDescription: A 6-step checklist for renewing your Canadian mortgage at the best possible rate and terms. Greater Vancouver-tested.
excerpt: Your renewal letter is an opening offer, not a quote. Here's the 6-step playbook to handle it properly.

body.heading: Step 1: Don't sign the letter
paragraphs: About 60% of Canadians sign whatever rate their existing lender mails them at renewal. That single decision routinely costs Greater Vancouver homeowners $5,000–$25,000 over a five-year term. Open the letter, note the rate and the maturity date, and put it aside.

body.heading: Step 2: Pull current market rates
paragraphs: A broker can quote you what your existing lender is offering brand-new clients today, plus what 5–10 competing lenders are pricing for your situation. The gap is usually 15–60 basis points lower than your renewal letter.

body.heading: Step 3: Push back at your existing lender
paragraphs: Before considering a switch, tell your current lender you've been quoted lower elsewhere and ask them to match. Most will drop their renewal rate by 15–30 bps when pushed. Some will match the broker quote entirely. About a third of my renewal clients stay with their existing lender at a meaningfully better rate after this single step.

body.heading: Step 4: Compare the all-in cost, not just the rate
paragraphs: Two lenders quoting the same rate can produce very different outcomes when you factor in: prepayment privileges, IRD calculation methodology, portability terms, payment frequency options, and skip-a-payment features. If there's any chance you'll break early, the IRD calculation matters more than 10 bps on rate.

body.heading: Step 5: If you switch, confirm zero out-of-pocket
paragraphs: On a straight switch (same balance, same amortization, no equity take-out), most monoline lenders cover discharge fees, legal fees and appraisal in their switch program. Total cost to you: zero. If a lender quotes a fee, push back or move on.

body.heading: Step 6: Lock 90+ days before maturity
paragraphs: Most lenders hold a new rate for 90 days. Lock as soon as you've decided. If rates drop during the hold, most lenders float you down to the lower rate; if rates rise, you're protected. There's almost no downside to locking early.

faqs.question: How early should I start?
faqs.answer: 120 days before maturity is ideal. The last 90 days are the negotiating window; before that you're just gathering information.
